enforcement 
322-O.Ut 
January 24, 1979 
Betty B. liar-ris 
504 North ALta Drive 
Beverly Hills, CA 
90210 
Dear Ms. Harris: 
Re: 
Opinion Request 
No. 78-016 
Conl1lct of Interest 
322-6.0U4 
This letter is in response to your letter of January 12, 
1979, in which you ask the Fair Political Practices Commission 
to reconsider your opinion request of December 14, 1978. 
Specifically, you ask the Commission to' rule on whe ther your 
actions with respect to the lawsuit filed by the League of 
women Voters against the City of Beverly Bills constituted a 
conflict of interest under the provisions of the Political 
Reform Act. 
The Commis.sion, at its meeting of January 17, 1979, 
declined to' answer this question in a formal published opinion, 
but we hope that the following advice will be helpful. 
The facts as r understand them are as follows. 
Recently, 
the Beverly Hills City Council removed you from the Traffic 
and Parks Commission for the City of Beverly Bills. 
You 
stated that your removal resulted from your signing of a 
declaration as a witness to a conversation related to a 
lawsuit brought by the Beverly Bills League of Women Voters 
against the City. 
Based on these facts, you did not have a conflict of 
interest within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. 
For a conflict of interest to exist under the Act, a public 
official must have a financial interest in a governmental 
decision. 
An official has a financial interest in a govern-
mental decision if the decision foreseeably would have a 
material financial effect on her investments, interests in 
real property or sources of income. 
Based on your assertion 
that you do not have a financial interest in any of the 


Betty B. Barris 
January 24, 1979 
Page Two 
entities or persons which are parties to the lawsuit in 
question, you do not have a conflict of interest' within the 
meaning of the Political Reform Act. 
The Commission's jurisdiction is limited to interpreting 
and enforcing the provisions of the Political Reform Act. 
Therefore,. this advice does not rule on whether your removal 
from office was proper or whether you may have a cQnflict of 
interest under any other statute or the common law. 
If you need further assistance, please contact Ted Prim, 
Chief of the Commission's Conflicts of Interest Division, or 
me. 
MB:TP:plh 
Sincerely,

Michael Bennett 
Executive Director 


JANUARY 121 1979 
MR. TED PRIM 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
P. O. Box 8071 
SACRAMENTO I CALIFORNIA 
DEAR r"R. PR IM: 
I HAVE APPARENTLY FAILED TO MAKE MY NEED CLEAR. 
I WANT YOUR COMMiSSION TO ~XPRESS ITS OPINION AS TO WHETHER I 
HAD A CONFLICT OF INTEREST. 
MR. STERN'S LETTER OF JANUARY 101 1979 DID NOT CONFIRM NOR DENY 
THE QUERY RAISED IN MY LETTER OF DECEMBER 141 1978. 
THE FACTS ARE I N MY OR I G I NAL LETTER •. 
IT IS IMPORTANT TO ME AND TO OTHERS WHO SEEK TO SERVE BEVERLY 
HILLS THAT IT BE ESTABLISHED THAT A CONFLICT OF INTEREST IS ABSENT 
WHEN A CITY OFFICIAL TESTIFIES TO UNDENIABLE FACTS IN A LAW SUIT 
AGAINST THE CITY. 
IF AN APPEAL IS NEEDED TO HAVE YOUR COMMISSION EXPRESS ITS VIEWS 
PLEASE CONSIDER THIS AN APPEAL. 
SINCERELY) 
J~ rJ..~ 
BETTY ~f HARRIS 


,« II 
Date Roc=i'l~d /;;t -/17-
gJ~1ff.~ 
fC4 JYo-d~ 9UAHJ. 
9~~~~ 
December 14, 1978 
Daniel Lowenstein, Chairman 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
P.o. Box 807 
Sacramento, California 
95814 
Dear Mr. Chairman and Members of the Commission: 
nesoon:;~ ':!.JQ /-;;; - P 
In accordance with Government Code §83114 I respectfully 
request an opinion as to whether there is a conflict of 
interest presented by the following. 
A resolution (Item A) was passed on December 12, 1978, 
removing me as a Traffic & Parking Commissioner of the 
City of Beverly Hills. 
This action was reported to have been prefaced by Mayor 
Joseph Tilem saying I was in an adversary position with 
the City. 
This, he said, had been brought to his attention 
by the Acting City Attorney, Jack Allen. 
This allegation 
resulted from my signing a declaration as a witness to a 
conversation (Item B) in a lawsuit by the Beverly Hills 
League of Women Voters against the City. 
It was the opinion of the City Attorney and the City Council 
-that this constituted a conflict of interest since the 
December 14, 1978 agenda of the Traffic & Parking Commission 
had an item about parking tL~-limits in the industrial area 
(Item C) .. 
This, the Mayor said, might affect Mr. Fred Nason's property, 
a co-litigant with the League of Women Voters. 
The City 
Attorney asserted that a conflict existed because I was a 
litigant in the suit and an active witness against the City. 
Enclosed is a copy of the complaint. 
I 
am not a litigant, 
although I am a member of the Board of Directors of the 
League. 
My declaration is that of a witness to a conversation. 
The 
lawsuit questions whether the City has made a gift of a thing 
of value to a private developer. 
It seeks no monetary recovery. 


Daniel Lowenstein, Chairman 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
December 14, 1978 
Page Two 
The T & P Commission is advisory only to the Council. 
The 
members receive no remuneration nor reimbursement for expenses. 
My Commission appointment states that I am appointed to serve 
for the prescribed term which, for me, ends December, 1981. 
My second question is: 
If there is a conflict of interest, is the appropriate 
response, abstaining, or my removal? 
I do not believe I have a conflict of interest of any kind. 
I would be happy to answer any questions you might have. 
I 
hope for an early opinion on this matter by your Commission. 
Sincerely, 
~ 
4.~~r;e;.

interest under any other statute or the common law. 
If you need further assistance, please contact Ted Prim, 
Chief of the Commission's Conflicts of Interest Division, or 
me.

sections.conclusion: Based on the assertion that the requester does not have a financial interest in any of the entities or persons which are parties to the lawsuit in question, the requester did not have a conflict of interest within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. For a conflict of interest to exist under the Act, a public official must have a financial interest in a governmental decision, meaning the decision would foreseeably have a material financial effect on her investments, interests in real property or sources of income.
sections.question_synthetic: Did Betty Harris have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act when she signed a declaration as a witness in a lawsuit by the Beverly Hills League of Women Voters against the City, particularly given that a Traffic and Parking Commission agenda item might affect a co-litigant's property?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that Harris did not have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act because she had no financial interest in the parties to the lawsuit. The Commission declined to rule on whether her removal was proper or whether she had a conflict under other statutes or common law, as its jurisdiction is limited to the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes multiple pages: FPPC response letter dated January 24, 1979 (declining formal opinion but providing advice); Harris's appeal letter dated January 12, 1979; and Harris's original opinion request dated December 14, 1978. All three letters are included in single document. OCR quality is good. Minor OCR artifact: 'Barris' appears in header but 'Harris' is correct spelling throughout document.

embedding.summary: Betty Harris appealed the FPPC's initial response to her opinion request regarding whether her signing a witness declaration in a lawsuit against Beverly Hills constituted a conflict of interest. The FPPC concluded she had no conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act because she had no financial interest in the case.
